turns.content: I'm so tired of my CI failing on missing arguments. It's like Python where you get a TypeError if I don't pass the right keyword, but here in Rust it just refuses to compile and I have no idea why. Can you explain how this is similar to that scripting pain?

That frustration comes from losing the runtime hint of Python's optional parameters. In Rust, you want to enforce those constraints at compile time so the build never fails because a user forgot a field. A full proc macro can generate a builder pattern that makes every required field mandatory until explicitly set.
I think I understand the idea but not how it actually works under the hood. Is this just like implementing a trait manually or something more magical?

It is neither manual nor purely magical; it's a full proc macro that runs during compilation to expand your derive attribute into actual code. You define the builder logic once in the macro, then every struct using #[derive(MyBuilder)] gets transformed into a fluent API with compile-time checks.
Okay so I'm writing this macro? Does it need to implement any specific traits or interact with serde_json::Value?

You will likely use syn crate to parse the input structs and quote crate to emit the builder code. You do not need serde_json::Value directly unless you want to generate serialization helpers, but the core logic stays within syn's AST representation.
This sounds complicated for my first Rust project. Can I just use existing crates instead of writing a custom derive?

There are crates like builder-pattern or similar that provide basic builders, but they lack the compile-time enforcement you need. A full proc macro gives you exact control over which fields must be present and how errors surface.
I still don't get why this matters for my CI pipeline. Isn't just adding a validation check enough?

A runtime check in your code will fail the build only if you run that specific function; but with our proc macro, any missing required field becomes an immediate compilation error, preventing broken configs from ever reaching CI.
Wait a minute. If I just add a validation method in my struct, why can't I do that instead of this whole macro thing?

Because you need the builder pattern itself to enforce the order and presence of fields automatically during construction. A simple validation method does not prevent users from skipping steps; our proc macro generates a builder where omitting required fields is literally impossible at compile time.
I see now, but what happens if I make an optional field? How do the macros know which ones are required?

The macro scans your struct definition and marks specific fields as required while leaving others optional. When a user tries to build without a required field, the generated code produces a compile-time error instead of silently accepting an incomplete config.

I've been wrestling with Rust's builder pattern in Java. I tried to use serde_json::Value and impl Serialize, but I need a compile-time error if fields are missing. I wrote a custom derive macro that generates a builder struct with required/optional fields using syn and quote crates. It works for basic types like String and i32, but fails when the user forgets to set an optional field at runtime.

The issue is that your macro generates logic to validate presence, but if you're checking fields via Option<T> or nullable types without explicit defaults, the compiler won't catch missing values at compile time. You need to ensure every field in your builder has a default value of None or 0 so the generated code can enforce presence checks. The derive macro should generate a Builder struct where each optional field is wrapped in an Option type with a default of None.

That makes sense, but my generated builder still allows incomplete objects to pass through. I'm using serde_derive for the final serialization, so maybe I should just rely on that? Or is there something wrong with how syn parses optional fields?

Relying solely on serde_derive won't work because it doesn't enforce runtime presence checks for optional fields. Your custom derive must generate a Builder struct where every field is explicitly typed as Option<T> with a default of None, and then you add a method like has_field() that returns true only if the value was set. This ensures compile-time safety via type system while allowing runtime validation.

I tried adding a method to check if all required fields are present, but my macro isn't generating it. Is syn parsing optional attributes correctly? Maybe I need to use derive macro instead of proc-macro crate?

You're using the right approach with a full proc-macro, not a simple derive attribute. The problem is likely that your syn parsing logic isn't handling optional attributes properly. You need to explicitly check for attributes like #[serde(skip)] or #[serde(default = "default_fn")] in your macro implementation. If you don't parse these correctly, the generated Builder won't include the necessary validation methods.
I added attribute parsing to my syn code, but now I'm getting errors from quote crate when generating the builder struct. Should I use a different macro generator or is there a simpler way?

The error likely comes from incorrect syntax in your quote::quote! macro expansion. You need to ensure all generated code uses proper Rust syntax with correct semicolons and braces. Instead of debugging quote errors, try simplifying the output first by printing the generated struct directly using eprintln! to verify it compiles before integrating into larger code.
After fixing the syntax, I realized my builder doesn't have a way to set optional fields. Should I add a default constructor or use a separate method for each field?

Add a default constructor that initializes all fields with None/0 values, then provide individual setter methods like set_name() and set_age(). This gives you flexibility to build objects partially while ensuring the Builder struct enforces presence checks via runtime validation.
I added setters but now I need to validate that all required fields are present before building. Is there a trait I can implement for this, or should I just add a build() method?

Implement a build() method on your Builder struct that returns the final object only if all required fields have been set. This acts as a compile-time and runtime guard against incomplete objects. You can also add an Option<Error> return type to indicate which fields were missing, but a simple bool or Result<T> is often cleaner.
I added the build() method but it returns a Result instead of just T. Should I use std::result::Result for error handling? Or is there a better pattern?

Use std::result::Result<T, BuilderError> where BuilderError contains details about missing fields. This gives you explicit control over error messages and avoids generic panic behavior. If you want simplicity, just return Option<T> with None indicating incomplete objects.
I implemented Result<T, BuilderError>, but now I need to serialize the final object. My serde_derive isn't handling optional fields correctly in the output JSON.

The serialization issue is likely due to how your generated struct defines optional fields. Ensure every field is wrapped in Option<T> and use #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")] on each one so only set values appear in JSON output.
I added the skip_serializing_if attribute, but now I'm getting warnings about unused imports in my syn crate. Should I clean up the macro or is this normal?

Unused import warnings are common when you're testing partial functionality. Clean them up by removing unnecessary imports from your macro's use statement, but don't worry if some are temporary until the full implementation is complete.
I cleaned up the imports and now my custom derive compiles without errors. But I'm still getting runtime panics when building incomplete objects. Is there a way to catch these at compile time?

You can't fully prevent runtime panics for missing optional fields, but you can add a compile-time check by using Rust's type system. If you define required fields as non-Option types and optional fields as Option<T>, the compiler will ensure required fields are always present at compile time.
